---
title: "Lab 4"
subtitle: "Two-Sample t-test on a Live A/B Simulation: ARPU of the GreenBowl App"
author: "Ihor Miroshnychenko"
institute: Kyiv School of Economics
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

# Objective

In **Lab 3** you ran a proper two-sample A/B test on a **proportion** with
the $Z$-test, where the variance was "self-describing" ($\sigma^2 = p(1-p)$).

**Lab 4** moves to a **continuous** metric — **ARPU** (average revenue per
user per week, in UAH) — where the variance is **unknown** and must be
estimated from the data as $S^2$. That single change ($\sigma^2 \to S^2$) is
what turns a $Z$-test into a **$t$-test**, with all the consequences you saw
in the lecture and in Practice 4: Student's distribution on small samples,
Welch vs. pooled, the CLT + Slutsky rescue on large samples, the MDE.

The twist: **you do not get to invent your numbers.** You will pull a
**live A/B dataset from the GreenBowl simulation platform**, keyed to your
own student id. The true data-generating parameters — the effect size, the
two variances, the group sizes, even *whether there is any effect at all* —
are **hidden from you**. Your job is to recover the correct business decision
from the data alone, exactly as a real product analyst must.

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Everyone's data is different — on purpose

Your dataset is unique to your id. It might contain a large effect, a tiny
one, or none. The variances of the two groups might be equal or wildly
different. The sample might be plenty or far too small for the conclusions
you want to draw. **There is no single "right answer" shared across the
class** — there is only the answer your data supports. Copying another
student's conclusions will be obviously wrong for your dataset.
:::

You will:

1. Pull your A/B dataset and **diagnose** it (shape, variance, sample size).
2. Wrap your test logic into a reusable `my_two_sample_t_test()` (Welch +
   pooled), verified against `scipy`.
3. Choose Welch vs. pooled **from your diagnostics**, run the test, and
   build a confidence interval **for the lift**.
4. Plan: compute power and the MDE you could realistically detect at your
   own sample size — and decide whether your experiment was even adequately
   powered.
5. Validate the infrastructure with a **two-sample A/A** tuned to *your*
   variance and sample size, and stress-test it if your data are skewed.
6. Write a stakeholder-ready recommendation memo.

# General Requirements

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## The fixed business context (same for everyone)

- **Metric:** weekly **ARPU** per user, in UAH — continuous, positive,
  right-skewed.
- **Baseline:** $\mu_0 = 250$ UAH (3 months of history for the old menu).
- **Cohorts:** `control` = old menu, `test` = new menu (combo deals).
- **Break-even = +15 UAH** — the **decision threshold**. Below this lift the
  extra revenue does not cover rebuilding the menu and logistics, so the
  feature is not worth shipping.
- **Design-MDE = +25 UAH** — the **planning threshold**. This is the "clear
  win" the team sizes the experiment to detect (power $0.8$). It is a
  *different* number from break-even and is used **only** for power/sample
  planning, never as the ship rule.
- **Direction:** we only ship if the new menu is *better* → one-sided test,
  $H_1: \mu_{\text{test}} > \mu_{\text{control}}$.
- **Significance level:** $\alpha = 0.05$.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Two thresholds, two jobs — do not collapse them into one
**Planning** and **deciding** answer different questions and cost
differently, so they get different numbers:

- **break-even (+15)** answers *"is the effect worth shipping?"* → the ship
  decision is made relative to it.
- **design-MDE (+25)** answers *"which effect did we size the experiment to
  catch?"* → it feeds the power/$N$ calculation only.

The classic mistake is to require the **CI lower bound to clear the
design-MDE**. That demands *proving* a +25 effect, while the experiment was
only built to prove "$> 0$" — so it rejects genuinely profitable features
(lift between +15 and +25). Decide relative to **break-even**; use the
**design-MDE** only to judge whether your $n$ was adequate.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Report Format

Submit the report in **one** of the following formats:

- **Quarto → PDF** (`.qmd` → `.pdf`)
- **Quarto → HTML** (`.qmd` → `.html`, hosted separately, e.g. on
  [Quarto Pub](https://quartopub.com/),
  [Posit Connect Cloud](https://connect.posit.cloud/), etc.)
- **Jupyter Notebook** (`.ipynb`)
- **Google Colab** (share the link with viewing permissions)

The report must contain both code **and** narrative text. Every section
begins with a written explanation, not code.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Restrictions on libraries

You may use `numpy`, `pandas`, `matplotlib`, `scipy.stats.t`,
`scipy.stats.norm`, and
`statsmodels.stats.weightstats` / `proportion_confint` for
**verification only**.

You **may not** use `scipy.stats.ttest_1samp` / `scipy.stats.ttest_ind` as
the **primary** test — only as a **verification step**. The core machinery
(statistic, degrees of freedom, $p$-value, CI, power) must be **your own
code**, built on `scipy.stats.t` (`t.cdf`, `t.sf`, `t.ppf`) and
`scipy.stats.norm`.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Honour rule on the data platform

The file `greenbowl_ab.py` contains the hidden truth in its source. **Do not
read past the `# === instructor section ===` line, and do not call
`_reveal`.** The point of the lab is to decide *without* knowing the answer.
Any report that quotes the hidden parameters instead of estimating them
scores **zero** for interpretation.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Deadline

📅 **June 10, 2026, 23:59**
:::

# Tasks

## Get Your Data {#sec-data}

Download `greenbowl_ab.py` (provided with the lab) into your working folder
and load **your** A/B experiment. Use **one** id consistently — your KSE
email is recommended.

```{python}
#| label: load
#| eval: false

from greenbowl_ab import load_my_ab, BASELINE, BREAKEVEN, DESIGN_MDE, ALPHA

STUDENT_ID = "your.email@kse.org.ua"   # <-- put YOUR id here
control, test = load_my_ab(STUDENT_ID)

print(f"control (old menu): n = {len(control)}")
print(f"test    (new menu): n = {len(test)}")
print(f"baseline mu0 = {BASELINE} UAH,  break-even = +{BREAKEVEN} UAH,  "
      f"design-MDE = +{DESIGN_MDE} UAH,  alpha = {ALPHA}")
```

::: {.border}
1. State your `STUDENT_ID` and report $n_{\text{control}}$, $n_{\text{test}}$,
   the two sample means, and the observed **lift**
   $\overline{\text{test}} - \overline{\text{control}}$.
2. Confirm in one sentence that you understand the **true parameters are
   hidden** and that your entire analysis is an *estimation* problem.
:::

## Diagnose Your Dataset {#sec-eda}

Before choosing any test, look at what you were handed. This section drives
every methodological choice you make later — do not skip it.

::: {.border}
1. **Plot** both groups: overlaid histograms (or KDEs) of `control` and
   `test` on the same axes, with vertical lines at the baseline
   $\mu_0 = 250$ and at each sample mean.
2. Report a small **diagnostic table** with, for each group: $n$, mean,
   $S$ (sample SD, `ddof=1`), and the **sample skewness**
   (`scipy.stats.skew`).
3. Answer, **with evidence from your numbers/plots**, the three questions
   that decide your method:
   - **Normality / skew:** how skewed is each group? Is it close to normal,
     or heavy-tailed?
   - **Equal variances?** Compute the ratio $S^2_{\text{test}}/S^2_{\text{control}}$.
     Is it near 1, or far from it?
   - **Sample size vs. the CLT:** given the skew you observe, is your $n$
     *large enough* for the $t$-statistic to be approximately normal
     (recall Practice 4: small $n$ + skew breaks the test)?
4. In one sentence each, write down the **consequence** of each answer for
   the rest of the lab (e.g. "test SD is 3× control SD and the groups are
   unbalanced → I must use Welch, not pooled").
:::

## A Reusable `my_two_sample_t_test()` {#sec-fn}

::: {.border}
1. Implement
   `my_two_sample_t_test(a, b, alternative, equal_var, alpha, conf_int)`
   with this contract (mirror Practice 4):
   - Convention: $T = (\bar a - \bar b)/SE$; `alternative` is about
     $a - b$. Pass `a = test`, `b = control` so `"greater"` means
     *test beats control*.
   - Supports `alternative` $\in$ `{"greater", "less", "two-sided"}`.
   - `equal_var=False` → **Welch** SE and Welch–Satterthwaite $df$
     (the default); `equal_var=True` → **pooled** SE with $df = n_a+n_b-2$.
   - When `conf_int=True`, returns a **dict** with at least: `diff`,
     `t_stat`, `df`, `p_value`, `ci_low`, `ci_high`. The CI must match the
     `alternative` (two-sided → two bounds; one-sided → one finite bound and
     $\pm\infty$).
2. **Verify** your implementation against
   `scipy.stats.ttest_ind(test, control, alternative=..., equal_var=...)`
   for **both** `equal_var=True` and `equal_var=False`. Show that the
   $t$-statistic and $p$-value match to at least **4 decimal places**. This
   is the only legitimate use of the built-in function.
:::

## Welch vs. Pooled, Test, and CI for the Lift {#sec-test}

::: {.border}
1. **Choose** `equal_var` based on @sec-eda, and **justify it in one
   sentence** using your variance ratio and group sizes.
2. Run `my_two_sample_t_test(test, control, alternative="greater",
   equal_var=<your choice>, conf_int=True)`. Report the lift, $t$, $df$,
   $p$-value, and the **one-sided 95% lower bound** for the lift.
3. **Show what the wrong choice would have done.** Run the *other*
   `equal_var` setting too and put both in a table
   (`method | df | p_value | ci_low`). In one paragraph, explain *which*
   direction the pooled SE is biased for **your** data and why — and whether
   the choice changes your conclusion.
4. **Two-sided sanity line.** Report the two-sided $p$-value as well and
   confirm $p_{\text{two}} = 2\cdot\min(p_{\text{greater}}, p_{\text{less}})$
   for your data.
:::

## Power and Sample-Size Planning {#sec-power}

::: {.border}
1. Implement `my_two_sample_t_power(mean_diff, sd, n, alpha, alternative)`
   (normal approximation, equal $n$, $SE_{\text{diff}} \approx S\sqrt{2/n}$,
   as in Practice 4) and a `plan_t_experiment(sd, mde, ...)` that returns
   the $N$ **per group** needed for power $0.8$ (bisection or the closed
   form).
2. Using $S$ estimated from your **pooled** data, judge adequacy against the
   **design-MDE = +25 UAH** (the planning threshold, *not* the break-even):
   - **Was your experiment adequately powered?** Compute the power your
     actual sample size had to detect the **design-MDE = +25 UAH**.
   - **Plan it properly.** How many users **per group** would you have
     needed for power $0.8$ at the design-MDE? Compare with the $n$ you
     actually got.
3. **The decision layer (the heart of the lab).** The ship decision is made
   relative to the **break-even = +15 UAH**, *not* the design-MDE. First
   quantify how profitable your effect is, treating it as
   $\text{effect}\mid\text{data} \sim \mathcal N(\hat\Delta, SE)$:
   - report $P(\text{effect} \geq \text{break-even}) =
     \Phi\!\big((\hat\Delta - 15)/SE\big)$ and the **expected net value**
     $\hat\Delta - 15$ UAH/user.

   Then classify *your* result into exactly one of:
   - **the lower bound clears the break-even (+15)** → confidently
     profitable → a real, shippable win;
   - **significant ($p<\alpha$) but the lower bound is below break-even** →
     a real positive effect whose business value is uncertain → lean on
     $P(\text{profitable})$ and expected value before shipping;
   - **not significant, and well powered for the design-MDE (+25)** →
     genuine evidence of *no* clear win → do not ship;
   - **not significant *and* underpowered for the design-MDE** → you simply
     do not have enough data to conclude — extend the test (state the $N$
     needed).

   State which case you are in **and the evidence that rules out the
   others**. (This is the crux: a non-significant $p$-value means very
   different things in the third vs. the fourth case; and being *below the
   design-MDE* is **not** a reason to kill a feature that clears
   break-even.)
:::

## A/A Validation (Tuned to Your Data) {#sec-aa}

::: {.border}
1. Run **$\geq 5\,000$** two-sample A/A simulations under $H_0$, generating
   both groups from a lognormal with **your** estimated mean $S$ and **your**
   group sizes (use `np.random.lognormal`; pick $\mu,\sigma$ so the mean
   matches $\mu_0$ and the SD matches your $S$). For each, compute the
   $p$-value with **your** `my_two_sample_t_test`.
2. **Plot the histogram** of the $p$-values and report the empirical **FPR**
   (fraction $\le \alpha$) with a 95% **Wilson** CI
   (`proportion_confint(method="wilson")`). Is $\alpha = 0.05$ inside it?
3. **Stress test for your situation.** If @sec-eda showed your data are
   **heavily skewed and your $n$ is small**, the $t$-test may *not* be
   calibrated. Re-run the A/A at your actual (small) $n$ **and** at a large
   $n$ and compare the FPRs. Does the CLT rescue you at your sample size, or
   not? If your $n$ is large, instead briefly explain *why* you expect the
   FPR to be on target regardless of skew (CLT + Slutsky).
4. **One product-specific failure mode.** Describe one realistic way an A/A
   could fail **for GreenBowl specifically** (not generic "wrong data") —
   e.g. "whales who order for a whole office are hashed into the same cohort
   by phone number". Predict what the $p$-value histogram and the FPR would
   look like.
:::

## Final Recommendation {#sec-final}

::: {.border}
Write a **single-page memo** (≤ 400 words) to a **non-technical** GreenBowl
PM. It must contain:

1. Observed ARPU for control and test, the **lift**, and the **95% CI for
   the lift** (state Welch or pooled, and why).
2. The decision — **ship / iterate / extend the test** — and the reason,
   framed as the **CI vs. the +15 UAH break-even** (and, in plain words, how
   likely the lift is worth it), *not* just the $p$-value. Do **not** judge
   the feature against the +25 design-MDE — that number was only for sizing
   the experiment.
3. Two honest caveats grounded in your own analysis, e.g. whether the A/A
   passed, whether the experiment was underpowered for the design-MDE, or
   whether skew on a small sample makes the $t$-test untrustworthy.

It must be readable by someone who has never heard the words "$p$-value" or
"Welch". You are graded on whether a real PM could act on it without
follow-up questions.
:::

# Grading {.unnumbered}

| Criterion | Points |
|---|:---:|
| Correctness of methodology | 3 |
| Interpretation of results | 3 |
| Code quality and organization | 2 |
| **Total** | **8** |

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Grading Details

**Correctness of methodology (3 pts):**

- `my_two_sample_t_test` implements all three alternatives, **both** Welch
  and pooled correctly, and matches `scipy.stats.ttest_ind` to 4 decimals
  for both `equal_var` settings (1 pt).
- The Welch/pooled choice in @sec-test is justified by the data and the
  one-sided CI for the lift is correct (1 pt).
- The power calculation and planned $N$ in @sec-power are correct, and the
  A/A FPR with its Wilson CI is computed correctly (1 pt).

**Interpretation of results (3 pts):**

- @sec-eda correctly diagnoses skew, variance (in)equality, and
  sample-size adequacy from the data — and each diagnosis is *used* later.
- @sec-power places the result in the **correct one of the four cases**
  (decision vs. break-even, adequacy vs. design-MDE) and rules out the
  others with evidence, including $P(\text{effect} \geq \text{break-even})$
  — this is the key deliverable.
- The memo gives a concrete, product-specific decision based on **CI vs. the
  break-even** (not the design-MDE), with honest caveats — not a slogan.

**Code quality and organization (2 pts):**

- `my_two_sample_t_test`, `my_two_sample_t_power`, and `plan_t_experiment`
  are single reusable functions used across all sections (not
  re-implemented inline).
- Plots have axis labels, titles, and legible legends.
- Narrative text precedes every code block; each section reads as an
  argument, not a dump of outputs.
:::

# Defense {.unnumbered}

During the defense (5–7 minutes) you will be asked *"What if?"* questions
that probe whether you understand **why** each piece exists, for example:

- *"Your $p$-value is above $0.05$. Convince me this means 'no effect' and
  not 'not enough data'. What number settles it?"*
- *"You chose Welch. Show me — live — what the pooled $p$-value would have
  been on your data, and explain which way it is biased and why."*
- *"Your 95% lower bound for the lift is, say, $+6$ UAH and the break-even
  is $+15$. The PM says 'but $p < 0.05$, ship it'. Give your one-sentence
  answer."*
- *"Why do we size the experiment for the +25 design-MDE but decide at the
  +15 break-even? What goes wrong if you instead require the lower bound to
  clear +25?"*
- *"Your data are skewed and $n$ is small. Your A/A FPR came out at, say,
  $0.071$. Is the $t$-test miscalibrated here, or is that simulation noise?
  Show me the Wilson CI and decide."*
- *"Switch your `alternative` to two-sided right now. By how much does the
  lower CI bound move, and does your recommendation change?"*

You may also be asked to **change one parameter live** (flip `alternative`,
toggle `equal_var`, halve $n$, double the MDE) and explain the resulting
shift.
